query: history of artificial intelligence pre-Shannon McCulloch Pitts 1943 Dartmouth conference Claude Shannon
answer:
Here’s a concise, date-anchored sketch of AI’s “prehistory,” the 1943 McCulloch–Pitts milestone, and the road to the 1956 Dartmouth conference, highlighting Claude Shannon’s role.

Long antecedents (before 1900)
- ca. 4th c. BCE–19th c.: Formal reasoning and automata ideas emerge: Aristotle’s logic; medieval/early‑modern automata; René Descartes’ mechanistic mind analogies (1637); Thomas Hobbes’ “reason is reckoning” (1651); Gottfried Leibniz’s vision of a universal calculus of thought and his calculators (1670s); Charles Babbage’s Analytical Engine (1837) and Ada Lovelace’s 1843 notes on symbolic manipulation.
- 19th c. logic-to-math bridge: George Boole (1854) creates algebra of logic; later extended by De Morgan, Peirce, Schröder, and formalized with Gottlob Frege’s Begriffsschrift (1879) and, later, Russell & Whitehead’s Principia Mathematica (1910–1913).

Early 20th century: formal limits and computability (1900–1939)
- 1900: Hilbert’s program (mechanize mathematics) frames the Entscheidungsproblem.
- 1931: Kurt Gödel shows inherent limits of formal systems (incompleteness).
- 1936: Alonzo Church (λ‑calculus) and Alan Turing (“On Computable Numbers…”) characterize effective computation; Church–Turing thesis coalesces.
- 1931–1935: Vannevar Bush builds the Differential Analyzer (analog computing).
- 1937: Claude Shannon’s master’s thesis links Boolean logic to relay/switching circuits—establishing how logical reasoning can be realized physically. This is a crucial pre-1943 bridge from logic to machines.

Neuroscience and mathematical biophysics precursors
- 1906–1930s: Sherrington’s integrative action of the nervous system; Lapicque’s all‑or‑none neuron model (1907); Nicolas Rashevsky’s 1930s work on mathematical models of neurons and networks—all pave the way for logical/neuronal abstractions.

The 1943 landmark: McCulloch & Pitts
- 1943: Warren McCulloch and Walter Pitts publish “A Logical Calculus of the Ideas Immanent in Nervous Activity.”
  - Contributions: a binary threshold neuron model; demonstrations that networks of such neurons can implement propositional logic; arguments that, with feedback and timing, such networks could in principle realize general computation.
  - Limits: no learning rule (plasticity comes later with Hebb, 1949).

Between 1943 and Dartmouth (1943–1955): key currents
- 1945: John von Neumann articulates the stored‑program architecture (EDVAC report); late 1940s–early 1950s work on automata and self‑reproduction.
- 1948: Norbert Wiener’s “Cybernetics” unifies control, communication, and feedback in animals and machines.
- 1948: Shannon’s “A Mathematical Theory of Communication” founds information theory (formal notions of information/uncertainty that later permeate AI and learning).
- 1949: Donald Hebb proposes a synaptic learning principle (Hebbian learning), seeding connectionism.
- 1950: Turing’s “Computing Machinery and Intelligence” introduces the Imitation Game (Turing Test) and anticipates machine learning/search.
- 1950: Shannon’s “Programming a Computer for Playing Chess” formalizes game-tree search ideas (Type A brute force vs. Type B selective search) and evaluation functions; the same year he demonstrates “Theseus,” an electromechanical maze‑solving mouse that learns via relay memory.

Dartmouth and the naming of AI (1955–1956)
- 1955: The “Dartmouth Summer Research Project on Artificial Intelligence” proposal coins the term “Artificial Intelligence.” Authors: John McCarthy, Marvin Minsky, Nathaniel Rochester, and Claude E. Shannon.
- Summer 1956: Dartmouth workshop convenes. It consolidates a research community and agenda (symbolic reasoning, search, learning). Attendees and visitors include McCarthy, Minsky, Shannon, Rochester, Oliver Selfridge, Ray Solomonoff, Trenchard More, plus work from Allen Newell and Herbert A. Simon (Logic Theorist, 1956).

Claude Shannon’s throughline
- 1937: Shows how logic is built from physical switches (practical path from reasoning to circuits).
- 1948–1950: Brings rigorous notions of information and uncertainty; pioneers heuristic search and evaluation in chess; builds a learning demonstration (Theseus).
- 1955–1956: Co-authors the Dartmouth proposal and co-organizes the meeting that formalizes AI as a field.

If you want, I can expand any segment (for example, deeper on pre‑1943 logic/computation, the exact contributions of the 1943 paper, or Shannon’s chess work and Theseus) or provide a reading list.
